Threat.value: Irritable behavior, angry outbursts, or acting aggressively

Memory.value: When I remember the frightening event I feel like it is happening right now.
When I think about the frightening event I can sometimes smell things that I smelt when the frightening event happened.
I remember the frightening event as a few moments, and each moment is a picture in my mind.

Negative Appraisals.value: I can't trust that I will do the right thing
I can't stop bad things from happening to me

Maladaptive Strategies.value: I put on loud music or TV

issues.explanation: 'Widowed' is implausible at age 19; widowed status is only considered plausible from age 22 and above.

vignette:
This 19-year-old non-binary African patient, who is divorced, presents with a marked post-traumatic stress picture following a police-related trauma. Their PCL-5 score of 55 is consistent with substantial distress and functional impairment. In daily life, they describe feeling on edge, with trouble falling or staying asleep, irritability, and difficulty concentrating. These problems have begun to affect routines, relationships, and their capacity to stay mentally present.

Certain situations reliably intensify their distress. Confrontations are especially activating, and even the sight of happy families can stir an acute sense of difference and loss. Being naked is also particularly unsettling. In these moments, their sense of immediate danger rises sharply, and they become more restless and guarded, often seeking ways to reduce the intensity of what they are feeling. They may smoke, put on loud music or the television, and wear heavy make-up, using these strategies to blunt the inner tension.

Their trauma memory is experienced as fragmented and vividly sensory. When they remember the frightening event, it feels as though it is happening again in the present. The memory comes in snapshots, each moment held as a separate picture, and sometimes includes an intrusive smell that matches what was present at the time. These episodes are closely entwined with their distress and tend to intensify their sense of being unable to cope.

They also hold strongly negative views of themselves in the aftermath of the event. They describe feeling that they cannot rely on themselves, cannot trust that they will do the right thing, and cannot stop bad things from happening to them. These appraisals sit at the centre of their experience, deepening their sense of threat and shaping how they respond when the memory is triggered. At the same time, the recurrence of the memory seems to reinforce these beliefs, leaving them stuck in a cycle of fear, self-doubt, and repeated attempts to dampen distress.

Overall, the picture is of a young person who remains highly distressed, hyperaroused, and preoccupied by a trauma that continues to intrude into the present. Their coping efforts provide short-term relief, but the combination of vivid re-experiencing, persistent self-doubt, and heightened threat has left them feeling increasingly constrained in everyday life and emotionally distant from others.
